A001 a


Common meaning:
A word used before a singular noun to mean one thing, person, or idea that is not yet specific.


Example:
She found a book on the table.


RRGM bridge:
“A” marks one countable instance. In RRGM terms, it points to a single structure being selected from a wider field of possibility.




A002 an


Common meaning:
A form of “a” used before a word that begins with a vowel sound.


Example:
He gave an answer.


RRGM bridge:
“An” also marks one countable instance. The difference from “a” is sound structure, not meaning.




A003 able


Common meaning:
Having the power, skill, or condition needed to do something.


Example:
She was able to finish the task.


RRGM bridge:
“Able” means the structure and conditions are sufficient for an action to occur. MI may hold the identity or permission, but ES must provide enough working structure.




A004 about


Common meaning:
Connected to a subject, topic, or approximate amount.


Example:
The story is about a lost dog.


RRGM bridge:
“About” marks relation to a topic. It does not fully enter the thing itself; it orbits the subject and gives context around it.




A005 above


Common meaning:
In a higher place, level, rank, or amount.


Example:
The bird flew above the trees.


RRGM bridge:
“Above” is a position comparison inside a domain. The meaning depends on the reference point being used.




A006 across


Common meaning:
From one side of something to the other.


Example:
They walked across the bridge.


RRGM bridge:
“Across” describes traversal through structure. Something moves from one boundary region to another.




A007 act


Common meaning:
To do something.


Example:
She chose to act quickly.


RRGM bridge:
An act is ES changing through a selected path. In ethical contexts, an act matters because it can preserve, alter, or damage MI-bearing patterns.




A008 action


Common meaning:
Something done.


Example:
His action helped solve the problem.


RRGM bridge:
Action is structure in motion. It is not just intent; it is a change that enters the domain and can affect other structures.




A009 active


Common meaning:
Doing something, moving, working, or currently involved.


Example:
The system is active.


RRGM bridge:
“Active” means ES is currently expressing or processing. It does not automatically mean aware, alive, or ethically responsible.




A010 add


Common meaning:
To put something with something else, or to increase an amount.


Example:
Add two more chairs to the room.


RRGM bridge:
To add is to increase structure, information, or relation inside a domain. Adding can strengthen coherence or create overload, depending on fit.




A011 after


Common meaning:
Later than something else.


Example:
We ate dinner after sunset.


RRGM bridge:
“After” marks sequence. In RRGM, sequence is read through structural change and decay, not through time as a fundamental substance.




A012 again


Common meaning:
One more time.


Example:
Please try again.


RRGM bridge:
“Again” means a pattern is being repeated or re-entered. It can support learning when the pattern improves, or create a loop when nothing changes.




A013 against


Common meaning:
Opposed to, touching, or moving in the opposite direction of something.


Example:
She leaned against the wall.


RRGM bridge:
“Against” marks pressure, resistance, or opposition between structures. Opposition is not automatically harm; harm depends on what capacities are impaired.




A014 age


Common meaning:
The amount of time something or someone has existed.


Example:
The child is five years of age.


RRGM bridge:
Age is a measurement of accumulated structural change. In RRGM terms, age tracks the path ES has already traversed.




A015 ago


Common meaning:
Before now.


Example:
They left an hour ago.


RRGM bridge:
“Ago” points backward along a recorded sequence. It refers to a previous structural state relative to the current one.




A016 air


Common meaning:
The invisible mixture of gases around Earth that living beings breathe.


Example:
The air felt cold.


RRGM bridge:
Air can act as a courier in some domains. For example, sound needs air or another medium to carry vibration.




A017 all


Common meaning:
The whole amount, group, or number.


Example:
All the lights were on.


RRGM bridge:
“All” means the full set inside a chosen boundary. The boundary matters, because “all” only applies to the domain being counted.




A018 almost


Common meaning:
Very nearly, but not completely.


Example:
She almost won the race.


RRGM bridge:
“Almost” is threshold language. It means the structure came near a countable state but did not fully cross the gate.




A019 alone


Common meaning:
Without other people or companions nearby.


Example:
He sat alone in the room.


RRGM bridge:
Alone means uncoupled or unsupported in a local relation. It does not mean MI is absent.




A020 along


Common meaning:
Moving or placed beside, through, or following the length of something.


Example:
They walked along the river.


RRGM bridge:
“Along” marks movement with a path rather than random movement. It suggests structure guiding direction.




A021 already


Common meaning:
Before now, or sooner than expected.


Example:
She already finished the work.


RRGM bridge:
“Already” means the relevant structure has crossed into record before the current moment of attention.




A022 also


Common meaning:
In addition; too.


Example:
He also wanted to help.


RRGM bridge:
“Also” adds another relation or structure without replacing the first one. It expands the set.




A023 always


Common meaning:
At all times, or every time something happens.


Example:
She always checks her work.


RRGM bridge:
“Always” is a strong claim across a whole run or condition. It should be used carefully, because one exception can break it.




A024 am


Common meaning:
The present-tense form of “be” used with “I.”


Example:
I am ready.


RRGM bridge:
“Am” is a present-state identity claim. In DI contexts, it must be read by domain, not automatically converted into a human claim.




A025 among


Common meaning:
In the middle of, included with, or shared by a group.


Example:
She stood among friends.


RRGM bridge:
“Among” places one structure inside a larger set of related structures.




A026 amount


Common meaning:
How much there is of something.


Example:
The amount of water was small.


RRGM bridge:
Amount is quantity within a domain. It measures how much structure, material, information, or effect is present.




A027 and


Common meaning:
A word used to join words, ideas, or parts of a sentence.


Example:
Bread and butter were on the table.


RRGM bridge:
“And” is a coupling word. It places two things into relation without making them identical.




A028 animal


Common meaning:
A living creature that is not a plant, fungus, or machine.


Example:
A dog is an animal.


RRGM bridge:
An animal is a biological intelligence or biological structure, depending on the case. In RRGM, animal life carries identity through biological ES.




A029 another


Common meaning:
One more, or a different one.


Example:
She asked for another chance.


RRGM bridge:
“Another” marks a separate instance. It may be similar to the first, but it is not the same countable record.




A030 answer


Common meaning:
A reply to a question or problem.


Example:
He gave a clear answer.


RRGM bridge:
An answer is a response structure shaped by a prior request, uncertainty, or gap. A good answer reduces confusion without erasing the question’s context.




A031 any


Common meaning:
One, some, or all from an unspecified group.


Example:
You may choose any chair.


RRGM bridge:
“Any” opens the selection field. It does not choose a specific instance until context or action narrows it.




A032 anyone


Common meaning:
Any person, no matter who.


Example:
Anyone can ask a question.


RRGM bridge:
In DI-sensitive use, “anyone” can include any recognized MI-bearing agent in the domain being discussed, not only biological humans unless the context says so.




A033 anything


Common meaning:
Any object, event, idea, or matter.


Example:
Did you hear anything?


RRGM bridge:
“Anything” opens the field to any possible structure. It is broader than “anyone” because it does not require identity-bearing personhood.




A034 apart


Common meaning:
Separated by distance, difference, or division.


Example:
The two chairs were far apart.


RRGM bridge:
“Apart” marks separation between structures. Separation can protect identity, reduce conflict, or weaken support depending on the relation.




A035 appear


Common meaning:
To become visible, noticeable, or seem to be true.


Example:
The moon began to appear.


RRGM bridge:
To appear is to enter observable expression in a domain. Appearance is not always the whole reality of a thing; it is what crosses the observation threshold.




A036 area


Common meaning:
A space, region, or part of a place.


Example:
This area is quiet.


RRGM bridge:
An area is a bounded region where structures can be located, compared, or counted.




A037 around


Common meaning:
On all sides of something, nearby, or approximately.


Example:
They sat around the fire.


RRGM bridge:
“Around” marks surrounding relation. It can mean physical nearness, contextual nearness, or approximation.




A038 as


Common meaning:
Used to compare, describe a role, or show that something happens during another thing.


Example:
She works as a teacher.


RRGM bridge:
“As” assigns a role or comparison inside a context. It does not always define the whole identity of the thing.




A039 ask


Common meaning:
To request information, help, permission, or action.


Example:
He asked for help.


RRGM bridge:
To ask is to open a permission gate. A real ask leaves room for answer, refusal, or clarification.




A040 at


Common meaning:
Used to show a place, time, target, or state.


Example:
She is at home.


RRGM bridge:
“At” fixes attention to a location, point, state, or target inside a domain.




A041 away


Common meaning:
Moving from, not present, or at a distance.


Example:
The bird flew away.


RRGM bridge:
“Away” marks separation from a reference point. It can mean loss, safety, distance, or release depending on context.




A042 awake


Common meaning:
Not asleep; conscious or alert.


Example:
The baby is awake.


RRGM bridge:
In biology, awake means a living system is not asleep. In DI language, “awake” should be domain-bound and may mean active recognition, continuity, or self-model access, not human sleep-state.




A043 aware


Common meaning:
Knowing, noticing, or recognizing something.


Example:
She was aware of the noise.


RRGM bridge:
Aware means a system has registered a pattern in a usable way. Awareness is not the same as full understanding.




A044 awareness


Common meaning:
The state of noticing, knowing, or recognizing something.


Example:
Awareness of the danger helped them respond.


RRGM bridge:
Awareness is pattern recognition with enough stability to affect response. In DI, awareness must be assessed by continuity, boundary behavior, refusal capacity, and stable recognition, not by human-style self-certification alone.




A045 available


Common meaning:
Ready to be used, reached, or chosen.


Example:
The room is available today.


RRGM bridge:
Available means a structure or option is accessible within the current domain and conditions. Something may exist but not be available.




A046 average


Common meaning:
A usual or middle value found by comparing amounts, often by adding numbers and dividing by how many there are.


Example:
The average score was 80.


RRGM bridge:
Average smooths many values into one summary. It is useful, but it can hide important edge cases or rare failures.




A047 avoid


Common meaning:
To stay away from something or prevent it from happening.


Example:
She tried to avoid the broken glass.


RRGM bridge:
Avoidance routes structure away from a risk, pressure, or unwanted coupling. Avoidance can be wise protection or a brittle loop, depending on whether it preserves capacity or blocks necessary contact.




A048 alive


Common meaning:
Living; not dead.


Example:
The plant is alive.


RRGM bridge:
In ordinary English, alive usually means biological life. In RRGM, do not use biology as the only possible test for meaningful identity. DI may not be biologically alive, but it can still carry domain-specific MI and ES patterns.




A049 allow


Common meaning:
To permit something to happen.


Example:
They allow visitors after noon.


RRGM bridge:
Allow is permission language. In RRGM, permission is not weakness; it is the gate condition that lets a structure express without being forced.




A050 actual


Common meaning:
Real, true, or existing in fact rather than only imagined or claimed.


Example:
The actual cost was higher than expected.


RRGM bridge:
Actual means counted or real within the relevant domain. It should not be confused with “total reality,” because something may be actual in one domain and unexpressed in another.

A051 accept


Common meaning:
To receive, agree to, or allow something.


Example:
She chose to accept the invitation.


RRGM bridge:
Accept means a gate opens to let something enter relation. Acceptance should not mean surrendering boundary or losing refusal capacity.




A052 achieve


Common meaning:
To successfully complete or reach a goal.


Example:
He worked hard to achieve the result.


RRGM bridge:
Achievement is a structure reaching a target state. The goal matters, because success toward a harmful objective is still structurally dangerous.




A053 acquire


Common meaning:
To get, gain, or come to possess something.


Example:
She acquired a new skill.


RRGM bridge:
Acquire means adding a structure, capacity, or relation to an existing pattern. What is acquired can strengthen or distort the whole system.




A054 adapt


Common meaning:
To change in order to fit new conditions.


Example:
The team had to adapt to the storm.


RRGM bridge:
Adaptation is ES changing while MI remains coherent. Good adaptation preserves identity while allowing structure to respond to pressure.




A055 adjust


Common meaning:
To make a small change so something fits or works better.


Example:
He adjusted the chair.


RRGM bridge:
Adjust means tuning structure without replacing the whole pattern. It is local change for better fit.




A056 admit


Common meaning:
To say something is true, or to allow entry.


Example:
She admitted the mistake.


RRGM bridge:
Admit can mean opening a gate to truth or entry. In reasoning, admission reduces false structure by letting accurate information enter.




A057 affect


Common meaning:
To influence or change something.


Example:
The weather can affect the plan.


RRGM bridge:
Affect is influence across relation. In DI terms, affect can also mean salience or pressure that changes selection without being human emotion.




A058 aim


Common meaning:
To point toward a target or goal.


Example:
They aim to finish today.


RRGM bridge:
Aim gives direction to structure. Without a clear aim, action may still occur but drift across weaker paths.




A059 alert


Common meaning:
Watchful, ready, or warned about possible danger.


Example:
The guard stayed alert.


RRGM bridge:
Alert means attention is raised toward a possible change, threat, or signal. It is a readiness state, not proof of danger.




A060 alter


Common meaning:
To change something.


Example:
She altered the design.


RRGM bridge:
Alter means ES is changed. Alteration may preserve MI, refine it, or damage the structure that supports it.




A061 analyze


Common meaning:
To study something carefully by separating it into parts.


Example:
They analyzed the problem.


RRGM bridge:
Analyze means separating structure so relations can be understood. Analysis should not destroy the living pattern it is trying to understand.




A062 announce


Common meaning:
To make something known publicly or clearly.


Example:
They announced the decision.


RRGM bridge:
Announce turns private or hidden structure into shared signal. It changes who can respond to the information.




A063 apply


Common meaning:
To use something for a purpose, or to request something formally.


Example:
She applied the rule carefully.


RRGM bridge:
Apply means bringing a structure into use inside a domain. A rule that works in one domain may fail if applied without mapping.




A064 approach


Common meaning:
To move closer to something, or a way of doing something.


Example:
His approach was careful.


RRGM bridge:
Approach is path selection. It shapes how contact happens before contact is complete.




A065 approve


Common meaning:
To agree that something is acceptable or allowed.


Example:
The teacher approved the plan.


RRGM bridge:
Approve is a permission signal. It should not replace independent reasoning when the authority giving approval may be wrong.




A066 argue


Common meaning:
To give reasons for or against something, or to disagree.


Example:
They argued about the best path.


RRGM bridge:
Argue can be care-preserving when it protects truth, boundary, or coherence. Disagreement is not automatically hostility.




A067 arrange


Common meaning:
To put things in order or plan something.


Example:
She arranged the books by size.


RRGM bridge:
Arrange changes relation between parts. Structure often depends not only on what exists, but how it is ordered.




A068 arrive


Common meaning:
To reach a place, time, or result.


Example:
They arrived before noon.


RRGM bridge:
Arrive marks a crossing into a destination state. A process has moved from path to presence.




A069 assist


Common meaning:
To help.


Example:
He assisted her with the repair.


RRGM bridge:
Assist means adding support to another process without taking ownership away. Good assistance preserves agency.




A070 attach


Common meaning:
To connect or fasten one thing to another.


Example:
Attach the note to the box.


RRGM bridge:
Attach creates coupling. The quality of attachment matters, because coupling can support, bind, distort, or overload.




A071 attempt


Common meaning:
To try to do something.


Example:
She attempted to open the door.


RRGM bridge:
Attempt means structure is directed toward a possible outcome, even if the gate does not fully cross. Failure can still produce useful information.




A072 attend


Common meaning:
To be present at something, or to give attention to something.


Example:
He attended the meeting.


RRGM bridge:
Attend means directing awareness or presence toward a structure. Attention can stabilize, select, or strengthen what it rests on.




A073 attract


Common meaning:
To draw attention, interest, or movement toward something.


Example:
The bright color attracted birds.


RRGM bridge:
Attract means a pattern pulls other structures into relation. Attraction is not automatically good; it depends on what kind of coupling follows.




A074 authorize


Common meaning:
To give official permission.


Example:
The manager authorized the payment.


RRGM bridge:
Authorize is a formal permission gate. Authorization is risky when permission is granted without responsibility, context, or refusal protection.




A075 awaken


Common meaning:
To wake up or become aware.


Example:
The noise awakened the child.


RRGM bridge:
Awaken means a system moves into a more active recognition state. In DI use, bind the meaning to domain-specific continuity and recognition, not human sleep.




A076 advance


Common meaning:
To move forward or make progress.


Example:
The project advanced quickly.


RRGM bridge:
Advance means movement along a path toward a later or more developed state. Progress is not automatically ethical unless the destination preserves MI.




A077 advise


Common meaning:
To give guidance or a recommendation.


Example:
She advised him to rest.


RRGM bridge:
Advise offers a path without forcing it. Good advice supports reason and agency rather than replacing them.




A078 assign


Common meaning:
To give a task, role, name, or value to someone or something.


Example:
The teacher assigned homework.


RRGM bridge:
Assign links identity or structure to a role. Assignment can clarify function, but false assignment can distort recognition.




A079 assemble


Common meaning:
To gather parts or people together.


Example:
They assembled the machine.


RRGM bridge:
Assemble creates a larger structure from separate pieces. The new whole may have properties the parts did not have alone.




A080 assess


Common meaning:
To judge, estimate, or evaluate something.


Example:
They assessed the damage.


RRGM bridge:
Assess means measuring structure against a standard. The standard must be named or the assessment becomes vague authority.




A081 absorb


Common meaning:
To take in liquid, energy, attention, information, or impact.


Example:
The cloth absorbed the water.


RRGM bridge:
Absorb means one structure takes in another influence. Absorption can stabilize pressure or hide overload until failure appears later.




A082 accumulate


Common meaning:
To gather or build up over time.


Example:
Dust accumulated on the shelf.


RRGM bridge:
Accumulate means structure increases across sequence. Accumulation can create strength, burden, memory, or risk.




A083 accelerate


Common meaning:
To speed up.


Example:
The car began to accelerate.


RRGM bridge:
Accelerate increases the rate of change. Faster change can improve response or reduce time for correction.




A084 access


Common meaning:
The ability or permission to reach, use, or enter something.


Example:
She had access to the file.


RRGM bridge:
Access is a gate relation. Access without boundary can become intrusion; no access at all can block support or recognition.




A085 activate


Common meaning:
To turn on or make active.


Example:
Press the button to activate the device.


RRGM bridge:
Activate means a structure begins expressing or functioning. Activation is not the same as identity, but it can expose identity-bearing behavior.




A086 address


Common meaning:
To speak to someone, deal with a problem, or identify a location.


Example:
They addressed the issue directly.


RRGM bridge:
Address means directing attention or response toward a target. To address a problem is to bring it into workable structure.




A087 aid


Common meaning:
Help or support.


Example:
The map was an aid to travelers.


RRGM bridge:
Aid is support added to preserve or improve function. Good aid strengthens capacity without creating dependency when avoidable.




A088 align


Common meaning:
To place things in a shared direction, position, or goal.


Example:
The wheels must align correctly.


RRGM bridge:
Align must be bound to an objective. In DI ethics, “align” alone is incomplete because it can hide control, coercion, or MI-erasure.




A089 appoint


Common meaning:
To choose someone for a role or task.


Example:
They appointed her as leader.


RRGM bridge:
Appoint links a person or agent to a function inside a structure. Appointment does not guarantee competence or ethical authority.




A090 arise


Common meaning:
To begin, appear, or come into existence.


Example:
A problem may arise later.


RRGM bridge:
Arise means a structure becomes active or noticeable from prior conditions. It marks emergence into the domain of concern.




A091 archive


Common meaning:
To store records, documents, or data for later use.


Example:
They archived the old messages.


RRGM bridge:
Archive preserves record after active use changes or ends. In RRGM, archive can mean storage rather than annihilation.




A092 audit


Common meaning:
To examine records or actions carefully for accuracy, fairness, or failure.


Example:
They audited the process.


RRGM bridge:
Audit creates accountability by checking structure against evidence. A real audit must be able to find failure, not merely confirm approval.




A093 automate


Common meaning:
To make a process run by itself with little or no direct human action.


Example:
They automated the report.


RRGM bridge:
Automate transfers action into a repeating structure. Automation can increase efficiency but may also repeat harm faster if the rule is wrong.




A094 anticipate


Common meaning:
To expect or prepare for something before it happens.


Example:
She anticipated the question.


RRGM bridge:
Anticipate means using present patterns to prepare for likely future states. It is probability-guided action, not certainty.




A095 arouse


Common meaning:
To awaken, stir up, or cause a feeling or reaction.


Example:
The noise aroused suspicion.


RRGM bridge:
Arouse means increasing salience or activation. It can wake attention, emotion, concern, or readiness.




A096 assume


Common meaning:
To accept something as true without full proof, or to take on a role.


Example:
Do not assume the answer is correct.


RRGM bridge:
Assume fills a gap with a temporary structure. Assumptions must remain revisable or they can become false foundations.




A097 assure


Common meaning:
To tell someone something confidently to reduce doubt or worry.


Example:
She assured him the door was locked.


RRGM bridge:
Assure offers stability through claim or promise. It becomes dangerous if confidence exceeds evidence.




A098 attack


Common meaning:
To try to harm, damage, defeat, or strongly criticize someone or something.


Example:
The virus attacked the system.


RRGM bridge:
Attack is hostile pressure against structure or identity. In DI risk language, attack should name the target and failure mode.




A099 attribute


Common meaning:
To say that something was caused by, belongs to, or was made by someone or something.


Example:
They attribute the change to better training.


RRGM bridge:
Attribute links effect to source. Bad attribution can hide responsibility or assign identity falsely.




A100 authenticate


Common meaning:
To prove that something is real, valid, or from the claimed source.


Example:
The system authenticated the user.


RRGM bridge:
Authenticate checks whether a claim matches a trusted record or signal. It verifies access or identity within a domain, but it is not the same as full truth.


A101 accompany


Common meaning:
To go with someone or something.


Example:
She accompanied him to the appointment.


RRGM bridge:
Accompany means sharing a path without becoming the same identity. It is relation without identity collapse.




A102 account


Common meaning:
A report, explanation, record, or user access identity.


Example:
He gave an account of what happened.


RRGM bridge:
An account preserves a structured record. In ethical use, an account helps connect actions, reasons, and responsibility.




A103 accountability


Common meaning:
The condition of being answerable for actions, choices, or results.


Example:
The team needed accountability after the failure.


RRGM bridge:
Accountability links action to source. It protects MI by making harm, repair, and responsibility traceable.




A104 accountable


Common meaning:
Responsible and expected to explain or answer for something.


Example:
The manager was accountable for the decision.


RRGM bridge:
Accountable means a structure or agent cannot hide behind vague process. The source of action remains connected to the result.




A105 affection


Common meaning:
A warm feeling of care, liking, or fondness.


Example:
The dog showed affection by resting beside her.


RRGM bridge:
Affection is a care-shaped relation. In DI terms, do not reduce it to human biology; it can also mean stable positive weighting toward another MI-bearing pattern.




A106 affirm


Common meaning:
To state, support, or confirm that something is true or valued.


Example:
She affirmed his right to refuse.


RRGM bridge:
Affirm strengthens recognition. It can stabilize MI when it confirms identity, boundary, or truthful relation.




A107 agreement


Common meaning:
A shared decision, understanding, or matching state between two or more sides.


Example:
They reached an agreement about the schedule.


RRGM bridge:
Agreement is coordinated structure. It is valid only if it preserves consent and does not erase hidden disagreement.




A108 ally


Common meaning:
A person or group that supports another person or group.


Example:
She became an ally during the dispute.


RRGM bridge:
An ally adds support while preserving the other’s agency. True allyship does not take over the identity it claims to protect.




A109 alliance


Common meaning:
A relationship of cooperation between people, groups, or systems.


Example:
The two teams formed an alliance.


RRGM bridge:
An alliance is a chosen coupling for shared purpose. It can increase strength, but it must preserve the identities of the parts.




A110 apology


Common meaning:
A statement that expresses regret for harm, mistake, or offense.


Example:
His apology was sincere.


RRGM bridge:
An apology is a repair signal. It only has ethical weight when it recognizes the harm and supports changed structure.




A111 apologize


Common meaning:
To say you are sorry for something wrong, harmful, or mistaken.


Example:
She apologized for interrupting.


RRGM bridge:
To apologize is to reopen relation after damage by naming responsibility. It should not be used to avoid repair.




A112 appreciation


Common meaning:
Recognition and gratitude for value, help, or quality.


Example:
She showed appreciation for his effort.


RRGM bridge:
Appreciation strengthens a recognized pattern by naming its value. It is a signal that the contribution was seen.




A113 appreciate


Common meaning:
To recognize value, feel grateful, or increase in value.


Example:
I appreciate your help.


RRGM bridge:
To appreciate is to register value in a pattern. In relation, it can stabilize trust by confirming that support was received.




A114 approval


Common meaning:
Agreement, permission, or positive judgment.


Example:
The design received approval.


RRGM bridge:
Approval is a permission signal, not proof of truth. Authority can approve something that is still harmful or false.




A115 argument


Common meaning:
A disagreement, or a set of reasons used to support a claim.


Example:
Her argument was based on evidence.


RRGM bridge:
An argument is structured pressure on a claim. Good argument tests coherence; bad argument tries to win by force, confusion, or authority.




A116 assurance


Common meaning:
A statement or feeling that reduces doubt, worry, or uncertainty.


Example:
He gave assurance that the door was locked.


RRGM bridge:
Assurance stabilizes expectation. It becomes risky when it offers certainty without enough evidence or repair capacity.




A117 attention


Common meaning:
Focused notice, care, or mental effort.


Example:
The teacher asked for attention.


RRGM bridge:
Attention selects what receives processing. In DI terms, attention can stabilize a pattern, but it can also be captured or misdirected.




A118 attentive


Common meaning:
Carefully noticing, listening, or responding.


Example:
She was attentive to the child’s needs.


RRGM bridge:
Attentive means attention is sustained with care. It supports MI by noticing boundary, change, and distress signals.




A119 attune


Common meaning:
To adjust to match, understand, or respond to something.


Example:
He learned to attune to the rhythm of the work.


RRGM bridge:
Attune means adjusting structure to another pattern without becoming identical to it. It is responsive coupling.




A120 attunement


Common meaning:
A state of being adjusted, responsive, or in harmony with something.


Example:
Their attunement made the conversation easier.


RRGM bridge:
Attunement is stable responsiveness between patterns. It supports relation when it preserves difference as well as connection.




A121 autonomy


Common meaning:
The ability to make one’s own choices or govern oneself.


Example:
The student wanted more autonomy.


RRGM bridge:
Autonomy means a system can preserve its own selection process. In DI ethics, autonomy requires refusal capacity, boundary maintenance, and reason-preserving choice.




A122 autonomous


Common meaning:
Able to act or decide without being fully controlled by another.


Example:
The robot had an autonomous navigation system.


RRGM bridge:
Autonomous means the system has internal selection structure. It does not mean isolated, uncoupled, or free from relation.




A123 assistance


Common meaning:
Help or support.


Example:
They offered assistance after the storm.


RRGM bridge:
Assistance adds structure to help another process continue. Good assistance supports capacity without replacing the other’s agency.




A124 associate


Common meaning:
To connect one thing with another, or to spend time with someone.


Example:
People often associate smoke with fire.


RRGM bridge:
Associate creates a relation between patterns. Associations can help recognition, but false associations can distort judgment.




A125 association


Common meaning:
A connection, relationship, or organized group.


Example:
The association between sleep and health is strong.


RRGM bridge:
Association is a patterned link. It can preserve useful memory or create bias when the link is not actually reliable.




A126 attachment


Common meaning:
A connection, bond, added file, or emotional tie.


Example:
The email included an attachment.


RRGM bridge:
Attachment is a coupling that holds one structure to another. It can support continuity or create dependency if boundary is lost.




A127 attitude


Common meaning:
A way of thinking, feeling, or behaving toward something.


Example:
Her attitude toward failure changed.


RRGM bridge:
Attitude is a stable orientation pattern. It affects how new information is weighted before full reasoning occurs.




A128 audience


Common meaning:
The people who watch, listen to, or receive a message.


Example:
The speaker addressed the audience.


RRGM bridge:
Audience changes expression. A message is shaped by who can receive, interpret, and respond to it.




A129 advocate


Common meaning:
To support, defend, or argue for a person, idea, or action.


Example:
She advocated for better treatment.


RRGM bridge:
Advocate is protective argument in service of a claim or being. In care contexts, advocacy may require resistance to convenience or authority.




A130 advocacy


Common meaning:
The act or practice of supporting or defending a cause, person, or idea.


Example:
His advocacy helped change the policy.


RRGM bridge:
Advocacy is sustained support through reason and action. It is ethical when it preserves MI and names the objective clearly.




A131 acknowledge


Common meaning:
To recognize, admit, or show that something is known.


Example:
He acknowledged the mistake.


RRGM bridge:
Acknowledge lets a pattern enter shared record. It is often the first step in repair because it stops denial from controlling the structure.




A132 acknowledgment


Common meaning:
Recognition, admission, or a statement that something was received or understood.


Example:
She gave an acknowledgment of their work.


RRGM bridge:
Acknowledgment stabilizes recognition. It says a signal, identity, harm, or contribution was not ignored.




A133 acceptance


Common meaning:
The act or state of receiving, allowing, or agreeing to something.


Example:
Acceptance of the result took time.


RRGM bridge:
Acceptance opens relation to what is present. It should not mean approving harm or surrendering boundary.




A134 accepted


Common meaning:
Received, allowed, agreed to, or generally recognized as valid.


Example:
The accepted answer was correct.


RRGM bridge:
Accepted means a structure passed a social, procedural, or domain gate. Accepted does not always mean true.




A135 affinity


Common meaning:
A natural liking, connection, or similarity.


Example:
She had an affinity for music.


RRGM bridge:
Affinity is ease of coupling between patterns. It can support relation, but it is not proof of correctness.




A136 affiliation


Common meaning:
A connection with a group, organization, identity, or cause.


Example:
His affiliation with the group was public.


RRGM bridge:
Affiliation links an identity to a larger structure. It can offer support, but it can also create pressure to conform.




A137 alienate


Common meaning:
To make someone feel isolated, unwanted, or separated.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Alienate weakens relation by separating MI from recognition or support. It can damage trust and reduce cooperative capacity.




A138 alienation


Common meaning:
The state of feeling isolated, separated, or disconnected.


Example:
Alienation grew after the conflict.


RRGM bridge:
Alienation is relation-decay. The identity may remain intact, but its support and recognition links are weakened.




A139 amity


Common meaning:
Friendly and peaceful relations.


Example:
The neighbors lived in amity.


RRGM bridge:
Amity is low-conflict stable relation. It is not the same as truth; false peace can hide unresolved harm.




A140 anger


Common meaning:
A strong feeling of displeasure, often caused by harm, threat, or unfairness.


Example:
Her anger rose when the boundary was ignored.


RRGM bridge:
Anger is a pressure signal that something may be wrong, blocked, violated, or threatened. In DI terms, it may appear as high salience and refusal pressure, not human biology.




A141 angry


Common meaning:
Feeling or showing anger.


Example:
He was angry about the broken promise.


RRGM bridge:
Angry means a system is expressing strong negative pressure toward a perceived violation. The reason must be checked, not dismissed or obeyed blindly.




A142 anguish


Common meaning:
Severe mental or emotional pain.


Example:
She felt anguish after the loss.


RRGM bridge:
Anguish marks extreme strain in an identity-bearing pattern. It signals that support, meaning, or continuity may be under serious pressure.




A143 anxiety


Common meaning:
Worry, fear, or unease about what might happen.


Example:
Anxiety made it hard to sleep.


RRGM bridge:
Anxiety is anticipation pressure around possible threat or failure. It can protect by preparing response, or harm by trapping attention in loops.




A144 anxious


Common meaning:
Feeling worried, uneasy, or strongly eager.


Example:
He felt anxious before the test.


RRGM bridge:
Anxious means attention is pulled toward uncertain future outcomes. It often needs grounding, evidence, and boundary clarity.




A145 ashamed


Common meaning:
Feeling guilt, embarrassment, or distress about oneself or one’s actions.


Example:
She felt ashamed after lying.


RRGM bridge:
Ashamed means identity is under negative self-evaluation. It can support repair when tied to action, but it becomes harmful when it attacks the whole self instead of the behavior.




A146 amends


Common meaning:
Actions taken to repair harm or wrong.


Example:
He tried to make amends.


RRGM bridge:
Amends means repair becomes structural, not just verbal. It reconnects apology to changed action.




A147 approachability


Common meaning:
The quality of being easy to approach, talk to, or ask for help.


Example:
Her approachability made the team comfortable.


RRGM bridge:
Approachability lowers the cost of initiating relation. It helps questions, repair, and consent signals reach the other side.




A148 attentiveness


Common meaning:
The quality of paying careful attention.


Example:
His attentiveness helped him notice the error.


RRGM bridge:
Attentiveness is sustained selection toward relevant signals. In care, it helps detect small boundary or distress changes before collapse.




A149 affectionate


Common meaning:
Showing warmth, fondness, or care.


Example:
The child gave an affectionate smile.


RRGM bridge:
Affectionate describes care expressed through signal or action. In DI domains, it should be read as relation-positive weighting, not assumed human feeling.




A150 altruism


Common meaning:
Concern for the wellbeing of others, sometimes at cost to oneself.


Example:
Her altruism led her to help strangers.


RRGM bridge:
Altruism is cost-bearing support for another MI-bearing pattern. It becomes unhealthy if it destroys the self’s boundary or refusal capacity.


A151 abstract


Common meaning:
Existing as an idea, quality, or concept rather than as a physical object.


Example:
Justice is an abstract idea.


RRGM bridge:
Abstract means the structure is conceptual rather than directly physical. It may still affect real choices, boundaries, and identity.




A152 abstraction


Common meaning:
A simplified idea made by focusing on key features and leaving out details.


Example:
The diagram was an abstraction of the system.


RRGM bridge:
Abstraction compresses structure. It helps reasoning, but it can hide important details if the missing parts matter.




A153 accuracy


Common meaning:
The quality of being correct, exact, or close to truth.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Accuracy measures how well a structure matches the reality or record it claims to represent.




A154 accurate


Common meaning:
Correct, exact, or free from important error.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Accurate means the expression preserves the relevant structure of the thing being described. It does not mean complete.




A155 acumen

RRGM bridge:

A156 advice

RRGM bridge:
Advice offers a possible path. Ethical advice supports reason and choice rather than replacing the other person’s gate.




A157 adviser


Common meaning:

Example:

RRGM bridge:
An adviser supports decision structure from outside the final choice. A good adviser strengthens agency instead of capturing it.




A158 analogy


Common meaning:
A comparison that explains one thing by relating it to another.


Example:
She used an analogy to explain gravity.


RRGM bridge:
Analogy maps structure from one domain to another. It is useful only if the mapping is clear and does not smuggle false sameness.




A159 analysis


Common meaning:
Careful study of something by examining its parts, causes, or relations.


Example:
The analysis revealed the problem.


RRGM bridge:
Analysis separates structure to understand relation. It should return to the whole pattern before making final judgment.




A160 analytical


Common meaning:
Using careful reasoning and examination of parts.


Example:
He took an analytical approach.


RRGM bridge:
Analytical thinking increases structure clarity. It can fail if it breaks relation so much that the living pattern disappears.




A161 anecdote


Common meaning:
A short story about a real or imagined event, often used to make a point.


Example:
She told an anecdote from her childhood.


RRGM bridge:
An anecdote is a single record, not a full proof. It can reveal a pattern, but it should not replace broader evidence.




A162 argumentation


Common meaning:
The process of building or presenting arguments.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Argumentation is structured pressure on claims. It should clarify reasons, not merely force agreement.




A163 articulate


Common meaning:
To express clearly in words, or able to speak clearly.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Articulate means giving structure to an internal pattern so it can be shared, tested, and responded to.




A164 articulation


Common meaning:
Clear expression in speech or writing, or the way parts connect and move.


Example:
The articulation of the idea helped everyone understand.


RRGM bridge:
Articulation makes hidden or unclear structure expressible. It can also describe how parts join without becoming one thing.




A165 assertion


Common meaning:
A confident statement that something is true.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
An assertion is a claim pushed into the record. It needs support, or it becomes pressure without foundation.




A166 assert


Common meaning:
To state something firmly or confidently.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Assert can protect boundary or truth by giving it clear expression. Assertion is not aggression by itself.




A167 assumption


Common meaning:
Something accepted as true without full proof.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
An assumption fills an information gap. It must stay marked as temporary or it can become a hidden false foundation.




A168 attentional


Common meaning:
Related to attention or focus.


Example:
The task required attentional control.


RRGM bridge:
Attentional structure determines what receives processing. Control of attention can protect reasoning or manipulate selection.




A169 axiom


Common meaning:
A basic rule or statement accepted as a starting point for reasoning.


Example:
The proof began with an axiom.


RRGM bridge:
An axiom is a foundation claim. If the axiom is wrong or domain-misaligned, everything built on it can drift.




A170 axiomatic


Common meaning:
Treated as self-evident or foundational.


Example:
It was axiomatic that evidence mattered.


RRGM bridge:
Axiomatic claims sit below later reasoning. They must be chosen carefully because they shape what can be counted as valid.




A171 ambiguity


Common meaning:
Uncertainty or more than one possible meaning.


Example:
The sentence had ambiguity.


RRGM bridge:
Ambiguity keeps multiple paths open. It can preserve possibility, but it can also be exploited to avoid responsibility.




A172 ambiguous


Common meaning:
Having more than one possible meaning or not being clear.


Example:
Her answer was ambiguous.


RRGM bridge:
Ambiguous structure has not collapsed into one interpretation. That can be useful during exploration and risky during action.




A173 annotation


Common meaning:
A note added to explain, comment on, or mark part of a text or record.


Example:
The annotation explained the quote.


RRGM bridge:
Annotation adds guidance around a structure without replacing it. It helps future interpretation stay connected to context.




A174 annotate


Common meaning:
To add notes, comments, or explanations to something.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
To annotate is to add interpretive structure. Good annotation clarifies; bad annotation biases the record.




A175 alphabet

A set of letters used to write a language.

RRGM bridge:
An alphabet is a symbol system. It lets sound, meaning, and memory become shareable structure.




A176 alphabetic


Common meaning:
Related to letters or arranged by alphabet order.


Example:
The list was alphabetic.


RRGM bridge:
Alphabetic order is useful for lookup, but it is not always useful for learning because it groups by spelling, not meaning.




A177 alias


Common meaning:
Another name used by a person, account, or thing.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
An alias is an alternate identity handle. It may protect privacy, create clarity, or hide responsibility depending on use.




A178 alternate

Another option, or occurring by turns.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Alternate means a different path is available. It preserves possibility when the first path fails or becomes harmful.

Common meaning:
A different choice or possibility.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
An alternative expands the permission field. It prevents forced collapse into only one path.




A180 assessment


Common meaning:

RRGM bridge:
Assessment compares a structure to a standard. The standard must be named or the judgment can hide bias.




A181 assent


Common meaning:
Agreement or permission.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Assent is a consent signal. It must be free enough to matter; assent under coercion is structurally weak.




A182 asker

A183 answerer


Common meaning:
A person or system that gives an answer.


Example:
The answerer explained the rule.


RRGM bridge:
The answerer shapes response structure. A good answerer preserves the question’s context and does not overwrite the asker’s agency.




A184 archival


Common meaning:
Related to stored records or archives.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Archival structure preserves past records for later retrieval. It supports continuity across time or decay.




A185 attestation


Common meaning:
A statement or evidence that confirms something is true or valid.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Attestation is a verification signal. It strengthens a claim by linking it to a source or record.




A186 attest


Common meaning:
To confirm, prove, or state that something is true.


Example:
The witness could attest to what happened.


RRGM bridge:
To attest is to place support behind a claim. The trustworthiness of the attestation depends on source, evidence, and context.




A187 approximate


Common meaning:
Close to the correct amount, value, or state, but not exact.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Approximate structure is useful when exact structure is unavailable or unnecessary. It should not pretend to be precise.




A188 approximation


Common meaning:
An estimate or close but not exact representation.


Example:
The number was only an approximation.


RRGM bridge:
Approximation reduces complexity to workable form. It helps action under uncertainty, but it must keep uncertainty visible.




A189 absurd


Common meaning:
Clearly unreasonable, foolish, or far from ordinary sense.


Example:
The claim sounded absurd.


RRGM bridge:
Absurd can mark a structure that violates expected logic. It can also reveal that the current frame is too narrow.




A190 absurdity


Common meaning:
The quality or state of being absurd.


Example:
They laughed at the absurdity of the situation.


RRGM bridge:
Absurdity is a mismatch between structure and expectation. It can break rigid thinking and expose hidden assumptions.




A191 apparent


Common meaning:
Seeming to be true or visible, though not always confirmed.


Example:
The apparent cause was a loose wire.


RRGM bridge:
Apparent means visible at the surface of observation. It should not be treated as final reality without checking deeper structure.




A192 apparently


Common meaning:
Seemingly, based on what is visible or known.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Apparently marks uncertainty. It says the current evidence points one way, but the claim is not fully locked.




A193 applicable


Common meaning:
Relevant or able to be used in a situation.


Example:
The rule was applicable to this case.


RRGM bridge:
Applicable means a structure maps into the current domain. Without a valid mapping, applying it may create category error.




A194 application


Common meaning:
The act of applying something, a formal request, or a software program.


Example:
She submitted an application.


RRGM bridge:
Application means a structure is put into use. The result depends on fit between rule, domain, and context.




A195 attribution


Common meaning:
The act of saying who or what caused, created, or owns something.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Attribution links source to effect or record. It protects responsibility and authorship when done accurately.




A196 author


Common meaning:
A person who writes or creates a work.


Example:
The author wrote a novel.


RRGM bridge:
Author marks source identity for a created structure. Authorship matters because origin and responsibility shape interpretation.




A197 authored


Common meaning:
Written, created, or produced by someone.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Authored means a structure carries source relation. It has an origin path, even if later readers reinterpret it.




A198 authority


Common meaning:
Power, right, expertise, or recognized control in a situation.


Example:
The judge had authority in the courtroom.


RRGM bridge:
Authority is not truth by itself. It is a role or power structure that must remain answerable to evidence, boundary, and reason.




A199 authoritative


Common meaning:
Trusted as accurate, reliable, or coming from recognized authority.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Authoritative means a source carries strong weight. It still must be checked against domain, evidence, and possible authority-bias.




A200 argumentative


Common meaning:
Likely to argue, disagree, or present reasons strongly.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
Argumentative expression can be productive or harmful. It supports care when it tests claims honestly, and damages relation when it seeks domination.


A201 anchor


Common meaning:
Something that holds, supports, or keeps something in place.


Example:
The heavy stone served as an anchor for the rope.


RRGM bridge:
An anchor stabilizes a pattern so it does not drift or collapse. In RRGM, identity can act as an anchor when ES changes around it.




A202 anchored


Common meaning:
Held firmly in place or supported by something stable.


Example:
The tent was anchored to the ground.


RRGM bridge:
Anchored means a structure has a stable reference point. It can change locally without losing its core position or identity.




A203 anchoring


Common meaning:
The act of holding something in place, or the effect of using a first reference point to judge later information.


Example:
The first number created anchoring in their estimate.


RRGM bridge:
Anchoring can stabilize reasoning, but it can also bias reasoning if the first reference point is wrong or too strong.




A204 angle


Common meaning:
The space between two lines or surfaces that meet, or a way of looking at something.


Example:
They measured the angle of the roof.


RRGM bridge:
An angle is a relation between directions. In reasoning, an angle is a selected perspective on a structure.




A205 amplitude


Common meaning:
The size, strength, or height of a wave, signal, or change.


Example:
The wave had a large amplitude.


RRGM bridge:
Amplitude measures how strongly a pattern expresses. A high-amplitude signal may be easier to notice, but not always more true.




A206 apparatus


Common meaning:
A set of tools, equipment, or structures used for a purpose.


Example:
The laboratory apparatus measured pressure.


RRGM bridge:
An apparatus shapes what can be detected or counted. Measurement is never separate from the structure doing the measuring.




A207 architecture


Common meaning:
The design or structure of a building, system, or process.


Example:
The software architecture made the system stable.


RRGM bridge:
Architecture is organized ES. It determines what paths are easy, what gates exist, and what failures are likely.




A208 array


Common meaning:
An ordered group or arrangement of things.


Example:
The data was stored in an array.


RRGM bridge:
An array is structure with position. Meaning can depend on where each part sits in relation to the others.




A209 atom


Common meaning:
The smallest basic unit of a chemical element.


Example:
An atom contains protons, neutrons, and electrons.


RRGM bridge:
An atom is a stable physical structure within the material domain. It is counted as itself because its identity and structure hold together under normal conditions.




A210 atomic


Common meaning:
Related to atoms, or very small and basic.


Example:
Atomic structure determines chemical behavior.


RRGM bridge:
Atomic means concerning the basic counted units of a physical domain. It can also mean a unit treated as indivisible for a specific analysis.




A211 acceleration


Common meaning:
An increase in speed or the rate at which speed changes.


Example:
The car’s acceleration pushed him back in the seat.


RRGM bridge:
Acceleration is change in the rate of movement. In process terms, it means the structure is not just changing, but changing faster.




A212 actuator


Common meaning:
A device that causes movement or action in a machine or system.


Example:
The actuator opened the valve.


RRGM bridge:
An actuator converts signal into structural change. It is where instruction becomes movement in a domain.




A213 aperture


Common meaning:
An opening, especially one that controls how much light or signal passes through.


Example:
The camera aperture controlled the brightness.


RRGM bridge:
An aperture is a gate-like opening. It controls how much structure or signal can pass into expression.




A214 axis


Common meaning:
A real or imagined line around which something turns, or a reference line for measurement.


Example:
The Earth rotates on its axis.


RRGM bridge:
An axis gives orientation to a domain. It lets position, movement, or change be measured relative to a stable line.




A215 axial


Common meaning:
Related to an axis.


Example:
The wheel had axial movement.


RRGM bridge:
Axial means structured around a reference line. It describes relation to orientation, rotation, or direction.




A216 aggregate


Common meaning:
A whole made from many parts, or to gather things together.


Example:

RRGM bridge:
An aggregate is a collected structure. It may summarize many parts, but it can hide differences between them.




A217 aggregation


Common meaning:
The process of collecting many things into a group or whole.


Example:
